words: I am,and I don't even remember it, but she remembered it, but

words: was and all of that. And they had taken a boy to—raise, they

words: hadn't adopted him and we had to help him get in their wood. He

words: but there was a big, high sidewalk where they had it built down

words: where they had to go down steps to get down to where the wood

words: was,, and he'd push us off of that big, high sidewalk. Well, I

words: was only-well, I started school that fall, I was about six years

speaker: AS
words: oldjbut my sister was a year and a half older. We had to help

words: but then we had to get shed off the sidewalk to get the wood.

words: something like that?

speaker: KP
words: more

speaker: AS
words: No, I worked outside after I was married than I did- at home,

speaker: AS
words: well, I was married when I was seventeen, but I had worked out,

speaker: AS
words: I made a living after I was fourteen; I worked at housework.

speaker: AS
words: I went to Lewiston and worked.

speaker: AS
words: And that's all I knew, just to do housework, work for somebody.

speaker: KP

speaker: AS
words: Yes, I had to live in. Of course, up here, they treat their hired

speaker: AS
words: help-I know we always did, anyway, keep 'em on the ranch and

speaker: AS
words: treat 'em the same as they did their family. But when you work

speaker: AS
words: in town some of those people serve their meals, and boy, you

speaker: AS
words: don't eat with 'em. You serve to them and then you eat in the

speaker: AS
words: kitchen. And they have a nice, big front room and so on, but you

speaker: AS
words: go on up to your own room after that. But later on my sister

speaker: AS
words: worked just across the street from where I worked and so we'd

speaker: AS
words: go to the show or something about twice a week* that's about all

speaker: AS
words: we could afford when we was aworkin'. Five dollars a week why,

speaker: AS
words: you did all of the housework, cooking and washing and ironing

speaker: AS
words: for five dollars, of course, five dollars went a lot further

speaker: AS
words: than it does now.

speaker: KP
words: But five dollars to do all the cooking and the housework.

speaker: AS
words: Do everything, and they didn't have electric washers than, when

speaker: AS
words: I was working. You had one- but I washed everything on the board

speaker: AS
words: but the flat pieces like the sheets and the tablecloths and the

speaker: AS
words: pillow slips, they sent to the laundry. And the other things I

speaker: AS
words: did.

speaker: AS

speaker: AS
words: herself and then she married a man who had a little bit. The

speaker: AS
words: lady where my sister worked she was more thoughtful. There is

speaker: AS
words: a lot of difference in women.

speaker: AS

words: machine and well then, they had to have help. And I could hire

speaker: AS
words: out for that.

speaker: AS
words: over by Moscow to harvest, he run a threshing machine there

words: for thp threshing part of it, threshing tender of the machine

speaker: AS
words: that threshes the grain and when he come home why she'd changed

speaker: AS
words: so much he took her right away to the doctor, but it was too

speaker: AS
words: late, she only lived about a month after that.

speaker: AS
words: so- She'd have spells when she'd be working in the garden and

speaker: AS
words: say, "That was just a bilious spell," was what she called it

speaker: AS
words: have too much. So, one time he was atellin' me that-about a

words: grade. Well, he didn't wait til the school was out, they moved

words: and-

speaker: AS

words: sick, I went down to the doctor and he thought that's what it

words: was but I never did go back. Later on I knew good and well it

words: was but you didn't go and get checkups all time; at least I

speaker: AS
words: way off there in the canyon somewhere cutting wood. Willard,

words: he hadn't even started to school til that next fall- oh, yeah,

speaker: AS
words: he had, too, he had started to school, but he wouldn't a been in

words: school that day, Saturday he was home. But I couldn't a sent hin

words: back there anyway, because he wouldn't have known where to find his

speaker: AS
words: dad. So, he come home that night, well, he thought I had three

speaker: AS
words: weeks that I should go yet, and he said, he bawled me out for

speaker: AS
words: sending for his sister, but she said if you want me again, Ada,

speaker: AS
words: you phone don't pay any attention to Fred. So, I went on, I

speaker: AS
words: was home all day alone Saturday and Sunday I got up and I got his

speaker: AS
words: breakfast, and we had another barn where we had stock down there

speaker: AS
words: down the lane from our place there, there was a big barn down

speaker: AS
words: there, he had his stock. And I was getting pretty sick and when

words: he come in the house he helped me fix the bed, and he started

words: phoning to get some help. Now, boy, he was agettin' excited!

words: didn't know what in the world to do with 'em! And Mrs. Stoneburger

words: was the lady that came later they picked her up, and

speaker: AS
words: when the doctor came all he done was come in and look 'em over

words: and everything was alright and he could go back to town. The

speaker: AS
words: mud was so thick and so deep and we had such terrible roads

speaker: AS
words: that they had to just come to this little town three miles from

speaker: AS
words: our place with a car and then they had to take the rig on from

words: there and his brother brought the doctor on in, but we didn't

speaker: AS
words: need him and Fred's brother-in-law got Mrs. Stoneburger and she

speaker: AS
words: came up. Well, she had the babies all dressed an everything

words: was over time he got there. He just as well stayed in town.

words: What?

speaker: AS
words: Delivered your twins' then.

speaker: AS
words: Well, he didn't have much to do about it.

words: Well, he caught 'em! (Laughter)

speaker: AS
words: He just asked me where this was at and that was at, that was all.

words: But he was really excited.

speaker: KP
words: Well, how about you?

speaker: AS

speaker: AS
words: It's the other members of the family. I think I'm tough, I'll

speaker: AS
words: come someday. My son-funny he likes to tease me, Fred did

speaker: AS
words: too. But anything that makes me mad they just keep saying that

speaker: AS
words: So, he got to teasing me after his dad died about u$r boyfriend

speaker: AS
words: I was at Lewiston; so here a while back why,Amy daughter was sick and I was down

speaker: AS
words: there sending the kids to school and taking care of them and he

words: says,"There's about three of those old guys that you chase around

speaker: AS
words: with there in Juliaetta settin' out on your porch there, om the

words: porch when I came down." "Well," I says, "you look again when

words: you go back and tell 'em if they're still there to stick around,

speaker: AS
words: I'll be back!" (Laughter) So, if he wanted to play the game

words: I'd play it with him.

speaker: AS
words: German, yeah. Oh, he'd get a little bit perturbed at me because

speaker: AS
words: I talked back to him. Maude wouldn't talk back to him, so him

speaker: AS
words: and her'd get along fine, but if I didn't like something I let

speaker: AS
words: him know it. They usually know what I think. Then he said some

speaker: AS
words: thing about- he liked Fred, that was my husband, he liked him

speaker: AS
words: alright and he thought the world of his Dad, he was a German

speaker: AS
words: all right. And my sister went with a fullblooded German the same

speaker: AS
words: as I did and she married one, later on, course I was married a

speaker: AS
words: quite a bit before she was and I said, "What's the difference?"

speaker: AS
words: 0n there was later years, I don't at first that there was. When

speaker: AS
words: they came here, well, even when Fred and I were married and I

speaker: AS
words: there at Cameron

speaker: AS
words: started going to the church that he went to that Lutheran church,

speaker: AS
words: they stuck pretty much to the German then, but you don't see

speaker: AS
words: that any more, they have married in. At first those older ones

speaker: AS
words: didn't want to marry anyone but a German and they didn't want

speaker: AS
words: 'em to talk anything but German around home. Some of those old

speaker: AS
words: folks that had been here for years never did learn to speak En

speaker: AS
words: glish. And that's a whole lot stubbornness, nothing else. But

speaker: AS
words: they are an awful stubborn breed of people as I'm concerned, I

speaker: AS
words: lived with one for forty*-four years !(Chuckles )

speaker: KP
words: Yes, you're an expert then.

speaker: AS
words: n Yeah, they are- you're not going to tell 'em anything. But they

speaker: AS
words: over in Germany they don't have the resources they have here and

speaker: AS
words: they have to do a lot of saving and they came here-these other

speaker: AS
words: people had come and homesteaded, well, they eventually got tired

speaker: AS
words: of it and they'd sell out to the Germans^ and the Germans they

speaker: AS
words: built it all up and built nice homes and stuff. They come back

speaker: AS
words: and look at it after they sold it and saw how it turned out and

speaker: AS
words: they didn't like it. But it was their own fault. They could a
